data.title: NYAE Group Seeks Valuation of Up to $30M in IPO
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

NYAE Group, an e-commerce platform for collectibles and artwork in Hong Kong, is seeking a valuation of up to $30 million in an initial public offering.

The company said it intends to issue 770,000 shares in the offering at $2 to $2.20 each, according to a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission on Wednesday. The IPO would raise $1.6 million at a midpoint of $2.10 a share, and up to $1.7 million at the top of the range.

NYAE has 12.4 million shares outstanding.

The company said it intends to list its shares on Nasdaq under the ticker NYAE.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Fitch Affirms Astrea 7's Class A-1 and Class B Bonds; Upgrades Class A-2 Bonds
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-19 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has taken the following actions on the ratings of Astrea 7 Pte. Ltd. (Astrea 7) as follows:

--SGD526 million class A-1 bonds affirmed at 'A+sf'; Rating Outlook revised to Positive from Stable;

--USD175 million class A-2 bonds upgraded to 'A+sf' from 'Asf'; Outlook Stable;

--USD200 million class B bonds affirmed at 'A-sf'; Outlook Revised to Positive from Stable.

Astrea 7 Pte. Ltd.

----Class A-1 Bonds SGXF50246630; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; A+sf; Rating Outlook Positive

----Class A-2 Bonds XS2480766158; Long Term Rating; Upgrade; A+sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----Class B Bonds SGXF67664494; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; A-sf; Rating Outlook Positive

Transaction Summary

Astrea 7 is a private equity collateralized fund obligation (PE CFO) sponsored by Astrea Capital 7 Pte. Ltd. (Astrea Capital), a subsidiary of Azalea Asset Management Pte. Ltd (Azalea), which is ultimately owned by Temasek Holdings (private) Limited. Astrea 7 owns interests in a globally diversified pool of alternative investment funds. The bonds issued by Astrea 7 are backed by the cash flows generated by the funds.

The transaction consisted of approximately USD1328 million net asset value (NAV) of funded commitments and USD131 million of unfunded capital commitments across 38 funds as of the May 13, 2025, determination date. As of the same determination date, the NAV of the portfolio was based on valuations as of Dec. 31, 2024, and March 31, 2025, adjusted for subsequent capital calls and distributions.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

The affirmation of the class A-1 bonds and the upgrade of the A-2 bonds reflects their improving loan-to-values (LTV) detachment points (net of reserves) of approximately 3% and 16%, respectively, of NAV, as of the May 13, 2025, distribution date and the bonds' ability to withstand Fitch's fourth-quartile launch year scenarios and rating sensitivity analysis. At these levels of LTV, the class A-1 and A-2 bonds can withstand significant declines in transaction NAV and distributions in Fitch's rating sensitivity analysis without breaching Fitch's relevant launch year scenarios at the 'Asf' rating level.

The class A-1 and A-2 bonds are rated one notch higher than the model-implied rating of 'Asf' primarily due to the bonds' low relative leverage and robust cushions in Fitch's modeling scenarios, as well as the strong portfolio and sponsor quality and transaction performance to date. The Positive Outlook on the class A-1 bonds reflects the expectation of the bonds being fully reserved by the next distribution period given the low LTV.

The affirmation of the class B bonds reflects their LTV detachment point (net of reserves) of approximately 31% of NAV, as of the May 13, 2025, determination date and the bonds' ability to withstand Fitch's fourth-quartile launch year scenarios and rating sensitivity analysis. However, the class B bonds are rated one notch below the model-implied rating of 'Asf' given the bonds' structural subordination resulting from the transaction's 50% LTV-deleveraging threshold ahead of the class A bonds' scheduled call date in May 2027. The Positive Outlook on the class B bonds reflects the view that the class B notes' LTV is expected to continue improving as the transaction de-levers and the time for principal distributions to be made on the class B bond approaches.

Thus far, Astrea 7's underlying fund investments have performed well, and significantly better than the stress scenarios run by Fitch in the rating analysis. Since inception through May 13, 2025, Astrea 7 received distributions of USD903 million and capital calls of USD211 million. As of May 13, 2025, the LTV detachment point (net of reserves) for the class A-1 bonds was 3% of current NAV compared with 20% at launch and 11% in the December 2024 review, the LTV detachment point for the class A-2 bonds (net of reserves) was 16% of current NAV compared with 29% at launch and 23% in the Dec. 2024 review, and the LTV detachment point for the class B bonds (net of reserves) was 31% compared with 40% at launch and 36% in the December 2024 sector review.

Astrea 7 has had sufficient cash distributions to cover expenses, interest payments, and capital calls thus far. As a result, there have been no draws on the transaction's liquidity facility.

Fitch believes Astrea 7's liquidity position is strong, which should allow it to continue meeting capital calls, expenses, and interest, even if distributions were to decline. In the 12 months through May 27, 2025, Astrea 7's liquidity needs included approximately USD30 million in capital calls, USD7 million in expenses, USD37 million in bond interest, for a total of USD74 million. Over the same period, liquidity sources included USD196 million of capacity available on Astrea 7's liquidity facility, and USD269 million of cash from distributions. Based on these figures, Fitch estimates the transaction's liquidity coverage ratio for a period of one year at 6.3x.

Fitch has measured the ability of the structure to withstand weak fund performance in combination with adverse market cycles. All classes of bonds are rated in the 'A' category, reflecting their ability to withstand fourth-quartile-level performance under Fitch's launch year scenario analysis.

Key structural protections for Astrea 7 include amortization triggers tied to LTV levels, reserve accounts for payment of the class A and class B bonds, a liquidity facility to cover interest, expenses and capital calls, currency hedges to pay interest and principal of class A-1 bonds denominated in Singapore dollars and hedge euro-denominated investment exposure, and long final maturities on the bonds to allow the structure additional time to potentially weather a down market.

The transaction manager (Azalea) has the capabilities and resources required to manage this transaction. Astrea 7 is the seventh in a series of similar transactions launched by affiliates of the sponsor.

The sponsor's and bondholders' interests are strongly aligned, as the sponsor holds the entire equity stake in Astrea7. Furthermore, the success of Astrea 7 would contribute to one of the sponsor's goals of development of investment products based on private equity funds in Singapore.

Fitch has a rating cap at 'A+sf' for PE CFO transactions to reflect the uncertainty related to investment performance and timing of cashflows, variability of asset valuations, and lags in performance reporting.

RESERVE ACCOUNTS

The transaction has made all scheduled reserve payments to date and accumulated roughly USD353 million of eligible investments in the A-1 and A-2 reserves accounts, accounting for approximately 62% of the rated class A bonds outstanding. The class A bonds will continue to be reserved against (subject to sufficient cash flows) according to the scheduled reserve payments, which are made on a straight-line basis until the A-1 and A-2 bonds' scheduled call date.

Although the full notional amount of the class A-1 bonds will be reserved prior to the class A-2 bonds (subject to sufficient cash flows), in an event of default prior to the scheduled call date these funds would not be earmarked solely for the benefit of the class A-1 bondholders. In an event of default and subsequent acceleration, all funds in the operating accounts, reserves accounts and bonus redemption premium reserves accounts will be applied following the post-enforcement priority of payments. In this priority of payments, the interest and principal of the class A-1 and class A-2 bonds are applied on a pro rata and pari passu basis.

If an event of default were to occur prior to the scheduled call date of the class A-1 bonds, the reserve accounts for both class A-1 and A- 2 bonds would be combined with the remaining cash in the structure and applied to the priority of payments.

However, Fitch views an event of default prior to the class A-1 bonds' scheduled call date as unlikely, as Astrea 7's expenses and interest payments are projected to be sufficiently covered by cash flows even in stress scenarios, and the presence of the capital call and liquidity facilities act as an additional buffer.

The ratings of the account banks and transaction counterparties are at or above the minimum direct support counterparty ratings as per Fitch's "Structured Finance and Covered Bonds Counterparty Rating Criteria — November 2023." The transaction documentation permits investments in the reserves accounts to mature as late as the scheduled call date, which significantly exceeds the maturity levels contemplated in Fitch's counterparty criteria as it relates to eligible investments.

To account for the significant long-dated exposure bondholders may have to investment counterparties, the ratings of the Astrea 7 bonds are capped at the ratings of investments/deposits in the reserve accounts, or the ratings allowed for investments/deposits in the reserve accounts by the transaction documentation, whichever is lower. Therefore, if a security in the reserve accounts is downgraded in the future below the ratings of the class A bonds, the ratings of the bonds may also be downgraded, depending on the materiality of the exposure.

COUNTERPARTY REPLACEMENT

Much of the counterparty replacement language in the transaction documents states that the issuer will make commercially reasonable efforts to affect a counterparty replacement within a specified time period. Although Fitch currently rates the counterparties of the transaction at or above the minimum counterparty criteria guidelines specified in Fitch's "Structured Finance and Covered Bonds Counterparty Rating Criteria." If a downgrade below these levels were to occur and the counterparty was not replaced in a timely fashion, Fitch would review the circumstances at that time to determine if a rating action would be warranted, which could potentially include capping the rating of the senior-most bonds then outstanding at the then current rating of the downgraded counterparty.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Cash flows coming in lower than model projections, creating an increased risk that the funds will not generate enough overall cash to repay the bondholders, or pay for capital calls, expenses and interest on time. However, Fitch views this as unlikely based on the class A and B bonds' performance in Fitch's rating sensitivity analysis under fourth-quartile modeling assumptions. In this analysis, the class A and B bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 15% distribution haircut, with approximately 46% and 15% of collateral value remaining, respectively, in the worst launch year;

--A material decline in NAV that in Fitch's view would indicate insufficient forthcoming cash distributions to support the bonds at the assigned rating level stress. However, Fitch views this as unlikely based on the class A and B bonds' performance in Fitch's rating sensitivity analysis under fourth-quartile modeling assumptions. In this analysis, the class A and B bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 30% NAV haircut, with approximately 33% and 8% of collateral value remaining, respectively, in the worst launch year;

--Significant depreciation of the euro versus the U.S. dollar, which could affect absolute returns and the U.S. dollar value of distributions. Payments on the currency hedges that are larger than anticipated may leave fewer funds available to pay interest on the bonds, fund the reserves account and meet capital calls, leading to increased reliance on the liquidity and capital call facilities;

--A rating downgrade of a counterparty may be linked to and materially affect the ratings on the bonds, given the reliance of the issuer on counterparties to provide functions, including currency hedging, providers of the liquidity facility and bank accounts;

--PE CFO obligations have many inherent risks that the ratings may be sensitive to including the uncertainty of distributions, less liquid nature of the underlying investments, the degree of transaction leverage and the subjective nature of NAV valuations.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Fitch has an 'A+sf' rating cap for PE CFOs. Therefore, positive rating sensitivities are not applicable for the class A-1 bonds until the bonds are fully reserved;

-- Fitch has an 'A+sf' rating cap for PE CFOs. Therefore, positive rating sensitivities are not applicable for the class A-2 bonds until the bonds are fully reserved;

--The class B bonds could be upgraded to 'Asf' if they pass Fitch's 'Asf' modeling scenario with larger cushions, LTV decreases materially and there is greater visibility on deleveraging.

BEST-/WORST-CASE RATING SCENARIO

International scale credit ratings of structured finance transactions have a best-case rating upgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a positive direction) of seven notches over a three-year rating horizon and a worst-case rating downgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a negative direction) of seven notches over three years. The complete span of best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings for all rating categories ranges from 'AAAsf' to 'Dsf'. Best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings are based on historical performance. For more information about the methodology used to determine sector-specific best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings, please visit https://www. tchratings.com/site/re/10111579.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

DATA ADEQUACY

As the timing and size of the cash flows is uncertain, Fitch used historical private equity fund performance data from a well-known third-party data provider, which covers all performance quartiles of the various fund strategies and vintages ranging from 1990 to 2025, to model expected distributions, capital calls and NAVs of the private equity funds.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

data.title: Fitch Affirms White Rose's Class A Notes
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-19 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed White Rose CFO 2023 Holdings, LLC's (White Rose) USD400 million class A notes at 'A-sf'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

White Rose CFO 2023 Holdings, LLC

----Class A 96451WAA9; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; A-sf; Rating Outlook Stable

Transaction Summary

White Rose is a private equity collateralized fund obligation (PE CFO) managed by Thrivent Financial for Lutherans (Thrivent). White Rose 2023 indirectly holds limited partner (LP) ownership interests in five White Rose Funds held by White Rose CFO 2023, LLC, a wholly owned subsidiary of White Rose. The notes issued by White Rose are backed by the cash flows generated by the funds.

The transaction consisted of approximately $708 million net asset value (NAV) of funded commitments and $64 million of unfunded capital commitments across 70 alternate investment funds, as of the July 31, 2025, valuation date. The NAV of the portfolio was based on valuations as of March 31, 2025, and adjusted for subsequent capital calls and distributions.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

The affirmation of the class A notes at 'Asf' reflects the notes' ability to withstand fourth quartile-level performance in the underlying funds under all 15 of Fitch's launch year scenarios. However, sensitivity analysis on the fourth quartile-level performance stresses indicates vulnerability at the 'Asf' rating level due to declines in valuations. The rating of 'A-sf' is one notch lower than the model-implied 'Asf' rating driven by the reduced resilience observed in Fitch's sensitivity scenarios as well as the qualitative review of the transaction. The qualitative factors negatively influencing the assessment include a relatively higher proportion in the portfolio of new fund managers and the unique structure of the White Rose Funds, which does not include the same limitations on activities as seen in other Fitch-rated PE CFOs. Conversely, Fitch views the diversified portfolio on a look-through basis, an experienced sponsor with alignment of interest, and the stepdown amortization schedule as positive and neutral factors. The Stable rating Outlook reflects Fitch's expectation that the notes will continue to pass Fitch's fourth-quartile-level 'Asf' stress scenarios with some cushions, as well as the anticipated de-leveraging of the notes beginning in February 2026.

To date, White Rose's underlying fund investments have performed well. Since inception through July 31, 2025, White Rose received distributions of $225 million and capital calls of $52 million. As of July 31, 2025, the LTV for the class A notes was 50% of current NAV, compared with 49% at launch and 48% in the December 2024 review. The transaction is structured to include an LTV ratio test that restricts distributions to equity. The ratio is initially set at 50% and steps down beginning in February 2026. In addition to the NAV of the underlying fund assets, a liquidity reserve account will be available to pay interest and other expenses. Fitch included the liquidity reserve account in the LTV calculation.

Fitch believes White Rose's liquidity position is strong. Thrivent is obligated to meet any short fall in capital calls, 4% of the outstanding note balance will be kept in the liquidity reserve account, and interest payments on the notes are deferrable if cash flow is insufficient. Remaining liquidity needs within the structure are relatively small and expected to be covered by distributions from the underlying funds as they become further seasoned.

In the 12 months through July 31, 2025, White Rose's liquidity needs included approximately $27 million in capital calls, $2 million in expenses, and $31 million in aggregate note interest, for a total of $60 million. Over the same period, liquidity sources included $16 million in cash reserves and $143 million of cash from distributions. Based on these figures, Fitch estimates the transaction's base case liquidity coverage ratio for a 12-month period at 2.7x. An additional source of potential liquidity is White Rose's right to require Thrivent to contribute capital to satisfy capital calls if there is a cash shortfall.

Key structural features for White Rose include quarterly step-down amortization triggers tied to LTV levels, a cash reserve account, an obligation by Thrivent to cover any capital call shortfall, and a long final maturity on the note to allow the structure additional time to potentially weather a down market.

Certain structural features of the transaction involve reliance on counterparties, such as Thrivent. The ratings of the notes could be negatively affected in the event that key counterparties fail to perform their duties. While Thrivent's credit profile is currently not a constraint on White Rose's ratings, a deterioration in Fitch's assessment of the credit quality of Thrivent may lead to a downgrade of the notes, absent other mitigants.

Fitch believes the transaction manager (Thrivent) has the capabilities and resources required to manage this transaction. The sponsor and noteholders' interests are aligned, as the sponsor holds 100% of the equity in White Rose 2023. Thrivent manages the White Rose Funds and has ownership interests directly in the White Rose Funds, which is atypical for a PE CFO and could introduce some potential for conflicts of interest.

Fitch has a rating cap at 'A+sf' for PE CFO transactions to reflect the uncertainty related to investment performance and timing of cashflows, variability of asset valuations, and lags in performance reporting.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--The ratings of notes may be downgraded if cash flows materialize at levels lower than those modeled in Fitch's stress scenarios on a sustained basis. The class A notes would be fully repaid assuming an additional 15% distribution haircut, with approximately 14% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year;

--A material decline in NAV that, in Fitch's view, would indicate insufficient forthcoming cash distributions to support the notes could also lead to rating downgrades. The class A notes would be fully repaid assuming an additional 20% NAV haircut, with approximately 12% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year. However, in a 30% NAV haircut scenario, the class A notes could be downgraded to below 'BBBsf';

--The ratings of notes may be downgraded if Fitch's assessment of Thrivent's credit quality falls below the note ratings due to the sponsor's role as contingent liquidity provider;

--A ratings downgrade of a counterparty, absent replacement or other mitigation, may also materially affect the ratings of notes given the issuer's reliance on counterparties to provide functions, including providers of the bank accounts.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--The ratings of notes may be upgraded if the notes pass Fitch's 'Asf' modeling scenarios with greater cushion, if they exhibit greater resilience under Fitch's rating sensitivities and if the LTV decreases materially. Any potential upgrade is subject to the 'A+' rating cap for PE CFO obligations.

BEST-/WORST-CASE RATING SCENARIO

International scale credit ratings of Structured Finance transactions have a best-case rating upgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a positive direction) of seven notches over a three-year rating horizon and a worst-case rating downgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a negative direction) of seven notches over three years. The complete span of best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings for all rating categories ranges from 'AAAsf' to 'Dsf'. Best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings are based on historical performance. For more information about the methodology used to determine sector-specific best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings, please visit https://www. tchratings.com/site/re/10111579.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

DATA ADEQUACY

As the timing and size of the cash flows is uncertain, Fitch used historical private equity fund performance data from a well-known third-party data provider, which covers all performance quartiles of the various fund strategies and vintages ranging from 1990 to 2025, to model expected distributions, capital calls and NAVs of the private equity funds.

data.title: Fitch Affirms Class A Astrea VI Bonds; Upgrades Class B Bonds
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-19 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has taken various rating actions on the outstanding classes of Astrea VI Pte. Ltd. (Astrea VI):

--SGD382 million Class A-1 bonds affirmed at 'AA-sf'; Outlook Stable;

--USD228 million Class A-2 bonds affirmed at 'A+sf'; Outlook Stable;

--USD130 million Class B bonds upgraded to 'A+sf' from 'Asf'; Outlook Stable.

Astrea VI Pte. Ltd.

----Class A-1 Bond SGXF34882443; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; AA-sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----Class A-2 Bond XS2308478515; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; A+sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----Class B Bonds XS2308495360; Long Term Rating; Upgrade; A+sf; Rating Outlook Stable

Transaction Summary

Astrea VI is a private equity collateralized fund obligation (PE CFO) sponsored by Astrea Capital VI Pte. Ltd. (Astrea Capital), a subsidiary of Azalea Asset Management Pte. Ltd (Azalea), which is ultimately owned by Temasek Holdings (Private) Limited. Astrea VI owns interests in a globally diversified pool of alternative investment funds. The bonds issued by Astrea VI are backed by the cash flows generated by the funds.

The transaction consisted of approximately USD684 million net asset value (NAV) of funded commitments and USD67 million of unfunded capital commitments across 34 funds as of the Sept. 4, 2025, determination date. As of the same determination date, the NAV of the portfolio was based on valuations as of March 31 and June 30, 2025, adjusted for subsequent capital calls and distributions.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

The affirmation of the Class A-1 bonds at 'AA-sf' reflects the fully reserved nature of the bonds and the linkage of principal and interest repayment risk to the minimum credit quality requirements of the eligible investments/deposits held in reserve. Eligible investments/deposits held in reserve for the Class A-1 bonds' principal repayment must be rated 'AA-', per the transaction documents, and mature no later than the scheduled call date. Undrawn amounts from the liquidity facility provider, DBS Bank Ltd. (AA-/F1+/Stable), are available to cover interest payments of the Class A-1 bonds if current distributions from the transaction portfolio are insufficient.

The affirmation of the Class A-2 bonds at 'A+sf' reflects the fully reserved nature of the bonds and the linkage of principal and interest repayment risk to the minimum credit quality requirements of the eligible investments/deposits held in reserve. Eligible investments/deposits held in reserve for the Class A-2 bonds' principal repayment can be held in entities rated 'AA-' and 'A+', per the transaction documents. The Class A bonds have been fully reserved (a full 12 months ahead of schedule) and are scheduled to be called March 18, 2026.

The upgrade of the Class B bonds to 'A+sf' reflects low LTV of approximately 17% of NAV, net of the Class A bond reserves as of the determination date, and the bonds' ability to withstand Fitch's fourth-quartile launch year scenarios and rating sensitivity analysis with substantial cushion. At this LTV, the Class B bonds can withstand a large decline in the transaction's NAV in Fitch's sensitivity analysis without breaching the relevant launch-year scenarios at the 'Asf' rating level. The Class B bonds are rated one-notch higher to the model-implied rating of 'Asf', given the expectations of rapid paydown of the Class B bonds, as the call date for the Class A bonds is less than six months away, after which 90% of available cash flow (excluding interest, fees, and expenses) will be directed to repaying the Class B bonds.

To date, Astrea VI's underlying fund investments have performed well, and significantly better than Fitch's stress scenarios. Since inception through Sept. 4, 2025, Astrea VI received distributions of USD1334 million and capital calls of USD121 million. As of Sept. 4, 2025, the LTV detachment points (net of reserves) for the Class A-1 and A-2 bonds were 0% of current NAV, compared with 20% and 35% at launch, respectively and in line with the December 2024 review; and the LTV detachment point for the Class B bonds (net of reserves) was 17%, compared with 44% at launch, and 20% in the December 2024 review.

Astrea VI has had sufficient cash distributions to cover expenses, interest payments, and capital calls. As a result, there have been no draws on the transaction's liquidity facility.

Fitch believes Astrea VI's liquidity position is strong, which should allow it to continue meeting capital calls, expenses, and interest, even if distributions were to decline. In the 12 months through Sept. 18, 2025, Astrea VI's liquidity needs included approximately USD16 million in capital calls, USD4 million in expenses, USD22 million in bond interest, for a total of USD41 million. Over the same period, liquidity sources included USD133 million of capacity available on Astrea VI's liquidity facility, and USD178 million of cash from distributions. Based on these figures, Fitch estimates the transaction's liquidity coverage ratio for a period of one year at 7.5x.

Fitch measured the ability of the structure to withstand weak fund performance in combination with adverse market cycles. All of the bonds are rated in the 'A' category, reflecting their ability to withstand fourth-quartile-level performance under Fitch's launch-year scenario analysis.

Key structural protections for Astrea VI include amortization triggers tied to LTV levels, reserve accounts for their payment of the Class A bonds, a liquidity facility to cover interest, expenses and capital calls, currency hedges to pay interest and principal of Class A-1 bonds denominated in Singapore dollars and hedge euro-denominated investment exposure, as well as long final maturities on the bonds to allow the structure additional time to potentially weather a down market.

Fitch believes the transaction manager, Azalea, has the capabilities and resources required to manage this transaction. Astrea VI is the sixth in a series of similar transactions launched by affiliates of the sponsor.

The sponsor's and bondholders' interests are strongly aligned, as the sponsor holds the entire equity stake in Astrea VI. Further, the success of Astrea VI would contribute to one of the sponsor's goals of development of investment products based on private equity funds in Singapore.

Fitch has a rating cap at 'A+sf' for PE CFO transactions to reflect the uncertainty related to investment performance and timing of cashflows, variability of asset valuations, and lags in performance reporting.

RESERVE ACCOUNTS

The transaction has made all scheduled reserve payments to date and accumulated roughly USD529 million of eligible investments in the Class A-1 and Class A-2 reserve accounts, accounting for 100% of the rated Class A bonds outstanding. The Class A bonds will be called and paid in full (PIF) on March 18, 2026.

Although the full notional amount of the Class A-1 bonds was reserved prior to the Class A-2 bonds (subject to sufficient cash flows), in an event of default prior to the scheduled call date these funds would not be earmarked solely for the benefit of the Class A-1 bondholders. In an event of default and subsequent acceleration, all funds in the operating accounts, reserve accounts and bonus redemption premium reserve accounts will be applied following the post-enforcement priority of payments. In this priority of payments, the interest and principal of the Class A-1 and Class A-2 bonds are applied on a pro rata and pari passu basis.

If an event of default were to occur prior to the scheduled call date of the Class A bonds, the reserves accounts for both Class A-1 and Class A-2 bonds would be combined with the remaining cash in the structure and applied to the priority of payments.

However, Fitch views an event of default prior to the Class A bonds' scheduled call date as unlikely, as Astrea VI's expenses and interest payments are projected to be sufficiently covered by cash flows even in stress scenarios, and the presence of the capital call and liquidity facilities act as an additional buffer.

The ratings of the account banks and transaction counterparties are at or above the minimum direct support counterparty ratings as per Fitch's "Structured Finance and Covered Bonds Counterparty Rating Criteria." The transaction documentation permits investments in the reserve accounts to mature as late as the scheduled call date, which significantly exceeds the maturity levels contemplated in Fitch's counterparty criteria as it relates to eligible investments.

To account for the significant long-dated exposure bondholders may have to investment counterparties, the ratings of the Astrea VI bonds are capped at the ratings of investments/deposits in the reserve accounts, or the ratings allowed for investments/deposits in the reserve accounts by the transaction documentation, whichever is lower. Therefore, if a security in the reserves accounts is downgraded in the future below the ratings of the Class A bonds, the ratings of the bonds may also be downgraded, depending on the materiality of the exposure.

COUNTERPARTY REPLACEMENT

Much of the counterparty replacement language in the transaction documents states that the issuer will make "commercially reasonable" efforts to affect a counterparty replacement within a specified period. Although Fitch currently rates the counterparties of the transaction at or above the minimum direct support criteria guidelines specified in Fitch's "Structured Finance and Covered Bonds Counterparty Rating Criteria," if a downgrade below these levels were to occur and the counterparty was not replaced in a timely fashion, Fitch would review the circumstances at that time to determine if a rating action would be warranted, which could potentially include capping the rating of the senior-most bonds then outstanding at the then-current rating of the downgraded counterparty.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Cash flows lower than model projections, creating an increased risk that the funds will not generate enough overall cash to repay the bondholders, or pay for capital calls, expenses and interest on time. However, Fitch views this as unlikely based on the Class B bonds' performance in Fitch's rating sensitivity analysis under fourth-quartile modeling assumptions. In this analysis, the Class B bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 15% distribution haircut, with approximately 57% of collateral value remaining, respectively, in the worst launch year.

--A material decline in NAV that in Fitch's view would indicate insufficient forthcoming cash distributions to support the bonds at the assigned rating level stress. However, Fitch views this as unlikely based on the Class B bonds' performance in Fitch's rating sensitivity analysis under fourth-quartile modeling assumptions. In this analysis, the Class B bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 30% NAV haircut, with approximately 45% of collateral value remaining, respectively, in the worst launch year.

--Significant depreciation of the euro versus the U.S. dollar, which could affect absolute returns and the U.S. dollar value of distributions. Payments on the currency hedges that are larger than anticipated may leave fewer funds available to pay interest on the bonds, fund the reserve account and meet capital calls, leading to increased reliance on liquidity and capital call facilities.

--A counterparty downgrade may be linked to, and materially affect, the bonds' ratings, given the issuer's reliance on counterparties for key functions, including currency hedging, the liquidity facility and bank accounts.

--PE CFO obligations have many inherent risks that the ratings may be sensitive to, including the uncertainty of distributions, the less-liquid nature of the underlying investments, the degree of transaction leverage and the subjective nature of NAV valuations.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--The rating on the Class A-1 bonds is linked to the minimum rating requirement for eligible investments/deposits, therefore positive rating sensitivities are not applicable;

--The rating on the Class A-2 bonds is linked to the minimum rating requirement for eligible investments/deposits, therefore positive rating sensitivities are not applicable;

--The rating of the Class B bonds is at the Fitch rating cap of 'A+sf' for PE CFO transactions, therefore positive rating sensitivities are not applicable. .

BEST/WORST CASE RATING SCENARIO

International scale credit ratings of Structured Finance transactions have a best-case rating upgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a positive direction) of seven notches over a three-year rating horizon; and a worst-case rating downgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a negative direction) of seven notches over three years.

The complete span of best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings for all rating categories ranges from 'AAAsf' to 'Dsf'. Best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings are based on historical performance. For more information about the methodology used to determine sector-specific best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings, visit https://www.tchratings.com/site/re/10111579.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

DATA ADEQUACY

As the timing and size of the cash flows is uncertain, Fitch used historical private equity fund performance data from a well-known third-party data provider, which covers all performance quartiles of the various fund strategies and vintages ranging from 1990 to 2025, to model expected distributions, capital calls and NAVs of the private equity funds.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

data.title: Fitch Revises Outlook on Astrea 8's Class A-2 Bonds ; Affirms Class A-1 and A-2
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-19 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed the ratings on Astrea 8 Pte. Ltd. as follows:

--SGD520 million class A-1 bonds at 'A+sf'; Outlook Stable;

--USD200 million class A-2 bonds at 'Asf'; Outlook Revised to Positive from Stable.

Astrea 8 Pte. Ltd.

----Class A-1 SGXPM06B2656; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; A+sf; Rating Outlook Stable

----Class A-2 SGXPM06BV5U6; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; Asf; Rating Outlook Positive

Transaction Summary

Astrea 8 is a private equity collateralized fund obligation (PE CFO) sponsored by Astrea Capital 8 Pte. Ltd., a subsidiary of Azalea Asset Management Pte. Ltd (Azalea), which is ultimately owned by Temasek Holdings (Private) Limited. Astrea 8 owns interests in a globally diversified pool of alternative investment funds. The bonds issued by Astrea 8 are backed by the interests in and cash flow generated by the funds.

The transaction consisted of approximately USD1354 million net asset value (NAV) of funded commitments and USD121 million of unfunded capital commitments across 38 funds, as of the July 7, 2025, determination date. As of the same date, the portfolio's NAV was based on valuations as of March 31, 2025, adjusted for subsequent capital calls and distributions.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

The affirmations of the class A-1 and A-2 bonds reflect their net loan-to-value (LTV) detachment points (net of reserves) of approximately 22% and 37%, respectively, of NAV, as of the July 7, 2025, distribution date. The ratings also reflect the bonds' ability to withstand Fitch's fourth-quartile launch year scenarios and rating sensitivity analysis. At these LTV levels, the class A-1 and A-2 bonds can withstand substantial decline in transaction NAV in Fitch's rating sensitivity analysis without breaching Fitch's relevant launch year scenarios at the 'Asf' rating level.

The class A-1 bonds are rated one-notch higher than the model-implied 'Asf' rating, primarily due to their senior claim to the transaction's reserves ahead of the scheduled call date, the bonds' low relative leverage, robust cushions in Fitch's base case modeling and rating sensitivities and the strong portfolio and sponsor quality. The class A-2 bonds are rated equal to the model-implied 'Asf' rating, given the time subordination of principal reserving relative to the class A-1 bonds and their resiliency under Fitch's rating sensitivities.

The Positive Outlook on the class A-2 bonds is based on the anticipated gradual deleveraging of the bonds and the continued improvement in base case and sensitivity modelling scenarios.

Astrea 8's underlying fund investments have performed well, and significantly better than Fitch's stress scenarios. Since inception through July 7, 2025, Astrea 8 received distributions of USD273 million and capital calls of USD56 million. As of July 7, 2025, the LTV detachment point (net of reserves) for the class A-1 bonds was 22% of current NAV, compared with 26% at launch and 26% in the Dec. 2024 review. The LTV detachment point for the class A-2 bonds (net of reserves) was 37% of current NAV, compared with 40% at launch and 40% in the Dec. 2024 review.

Astrea 8 has had sufficient cash distributions to cover expenses, interest payments, and capital calls. As a result, there have been no draws on the transaction's liquidity facility.

Fitch believes Astrea 8's liquidity position is strong, which should allow it to continue meeting capital calls, expenses, and interest, even if distributions were to decline. In the 12 months through July 21, 2025, Astrea 8's liquidity needs included approximately USD56 million in capital calls, USD6 million in expenses, USD30 million in bond interest, for a total of USD92 million. Over the same period, liquidity sources included USD191 million of capacity available on Astrea 8's liquidity facility, and USD273 million of cash from distributions. Based on these figures, Fitch estimates the transaction's liquidity coverage ratio for a period of one year at 5.0x.

Fitch has measured the ability of the structure to withstand weak fund performance in combination with adverse market cycles. Both, the A-1 and A-2 bonds, are rated in the 'A' category, reflecting their ability to withstand fourth-quartile-level performance in the underlying funds under Fitch's launch-year scenario analysis.

Key structural protections include amortization triggers tied to LTV levels; reserve accounts to pay the class A bonds; a liquidity facility to cover interest, expenses and capital calls; currency hedges to pay interest and principal on class A-1 bonds denominated in SGD and to hedge euro-denominated investment exposure; and long final maturities on to allow time to potentially weather a down market.

The transaction manager (Azalea) has the capabilities and resources required to manage this transaction. Astrea 8 is the eighth in a series of similar transactions launched by affiliates of the sponsor.

The sponsor's and bondholders' interests are strongly aligned, as the sponsor holds the entire equity stake in Astrea 8. Further, the success of Astrea 8 would contribute to one of the sponsor's goals of developing investment products based on private equity funds in Singapore.

Fitch has 'A+sf' rating cap for PE CFO transactions to reflect the uncertainty related to investment performance and timing of cashflows, variability of asset valuations, and lags in performance reporting.

Reserve Accounts

The transaction has made all scheduled reserve payments to date and accumulated roughly USD114 million of eligible investments/banker's guarantee in the A-1 and A-2 reserves accounts, accounting for approximately 19% of the rated class A bonds outstanding. The class A bonds will continue to be reserved against (subject to sufficient cash flows) according to the scheduled reserve payments, which are made on a straight-line basis until the A-1 and A-2 bonds 'scheduled call date.

Although the full notional amount of the class A-1 bonds will be reserved prior to the class A-2 bonds (subject to sufficient cash flows), in an event of default prior to the scheduled call date, these funds would not be earmarked solely for the benefit of the class A-1 bondholders. In an event of default and subsequent acceleration, all funds in the operating accounts, reserves accounts and bonus redemption premium reserves accounts will be applied following the post-enforcement priority of payments. In this priority of payments, the interest and principal of the class A-1 and class A-2 bonds are applied on a pro-rata and pari passu basis.

If an event of default were to occur prior to the scheduled call date of the class A-1 bonds, the reserve accounts for both class A-1 and A- 2 bonds would be combined with the remaining cash in the structure and applied to the priority of payments.

However, Fitch views an event of default prior to the class A-1 bonds' scheduled call date as unlikely, as Astrea 8's expenses and interest payments are projected to be sufficiently covered by cash flows even in stress scenarios, and the presence of the capital call and liquidity facilities act as an additional buffer.

The ratings for the account banks and transaction counterparties are at or above the minimum direct support counterparty ratings per Fitch's "Structured Finance and Covered Bonds Counterparty Rating Criteria". The transaction documentation permits investments in the reserves accounts to mature as late as the scheduled call date, which significantly exceeds the maturity levels contemplated in Fitch's counterparty criteria as it relates to eligible investments.

To account for the significant long-dated exposure bondholders may have to investment counterparties, the ratings of the Astrea 8 bonds are capped at the ratings of investments/deposits in the reserve accounts, or the ratings allowed for investments/deposits in the reserve accounts by the transaction documentation, whichever is lower. Therefore, if a security in the reserve accounts is downgraded in the future below the ratings of the class A bonds, the ratings of the bonds may also be downgraded, depending on the materiality of the exposure.

Counterparty Replacement

Much of the counterparty replacement language in the transaction documents states that the issuer will make "commercially reasonable" efforts to affect a counterparty replacement within a specified time period. Although Fitch rates the counterparties at or above the minimum direct support criteria levels in Fitch's "Structured Finance and Covered Bonds Counterparty Rating Criteria," if a downgrade below these levels occurs and the counterparty is not replaced in a time, Fitch will review the circumstances to determine whether a rating action is warranted, which could potentially include capping the rating of the senior-most bonds outstanding at the current rating of the downgraded counterparty.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Cash flows coming in lower than model projections, creating an increased risk that the funds will not generate enough overall cash to repay the bondholders, or pay for capital calls, expenses and interest on time. In this analysis, the class A-1 bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 15% distribution haircut, with approximately 25% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year. The class A-2 bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 10% distribution haircut, with approximately 15% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year. However, an additional 15% distribution haircut for the class A-2 bonds would imply a rating of 'BBBsf' or lower.

--A material decline in NAV that in Fitch's view would indicate insufficient forthcoming cash distributions to support the bonds at the assigned rating level stress. In this analysis, the class A-1 bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 30% NAV haircut, with approximately 18% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year. The class A-2 bonds would be fully repaid assuming an additional 20% NAV haircut, with approximately 13% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year. However, an additional 30% NAV haircut for the class A-2 bonds would imply a rating of 'BBBsf' or lower.

--Significant depreciation of the euro versus the U.S. dollar, which could affect absolute returns and the U.S. dollar value of distributions. Payments on the currency hedges that are larger than anticipated may leave fewer funds available to pay interest on the bonds, fund the reserves account and meet capital calls, leading to increased reliance on the liquidity and capital call facilities.

--A rating downgrade of a counterparty may be linked to and materially affect the ratings on the bonds, given the reliance of the issuer on counterparties to provide functions, including currency hedging, providers of the liquidity facility and bank accounts.

--PE CFO obligations have many inherent risks that the ratings may be sensitive to, including the uncertainty of distributions, less liquid nature of the underlying investments, the degree of transaction leverage and the subjective nature of NAV valuations.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Fitch has an 'A+sf' rating cap for PE CFOs. Therefore, positive rating sensitivities are not applicable for the class A-1 bonds until the bonds are fully reserved.

--The class A-2 bonds could be upgraded if the bonds' LTV net of reserves decreases further, and the notes continue to pass the 'Asf' modelling scenarios with sufficient cushion.

Best/Worst Case Rating Scenario

International scale credit ratings of Structured Finance transactions have a best-case rating upgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a positive direction) of seven notches over a three-year rating horizon; and a worst-case rating downgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a negative direction) of seven notches over three years.

The complete span of best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings for all rating categories ranges from 'AAAsf' to 'Dsf'. Best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings are based on historical performance. For more information about the methodology used to determine sector-specific best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings, visit https://www. tchratings.com/site/re/10111579.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

DATA ADEQUACY

As the timing and size of the cash flows is uncertain, Fitch used historical private equity fund performance data from a well-known third-party data provider, which covers all performance quartiles of the various fund strategies and vintages ranging from 1990 to 2025, to model expected distributions, capital calls and NAVs of the private equity funds.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

data.title: Fitch Upgrades Nassau 2019's Notes; Affirms Nassau 2019's Liquidity Facility
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-19 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has taken the following actions on the Nassau 2019 CFO LLC (Nassau 2019):

--$20.0 million undrawn liquidity facility affirmed at 'A+sf'; Outlook Stable;

--USD51 million class A notes upgraded to 'BBB+sf' from 'BBBsf'; Outlook Positive;

--USD32 million class B notes upgraded to 'BBsf' from 'BB-sf'; Outlook Stable.

Nassau 2019 CFO LLC

----Class A 63172DAA9; Long Term Rating; Upgrade; BBB+sf; Rating Outlook Positive

----Class B 63172DAB7; Long Term Rating; Upgrade; BBsf; Rating Outlook Stable

----Liquidity Loans ; Long Term Rating; Affirmed; A+sf; Rating Outlook Stable

Transaction Summary

Nassau 2019 is a private equity collateralized fund obligation (PE CFO) managed by Nassau Alternative Investments (NAI). The manager is an affiliate of Nassau Financial Group. Nassau 2019 owns interests in a diversified pool of alternative investment funds. The issuance consists of notes backed by the ownership interests in, and cash flows generated by, the funds.

The transaction consisted of approximately $126 million net asset value (NAV) of funded commitments and $45 million of unfunded capital commitments across 97 funds as of the July 31, 2025, valuation date. The NAV of the portfolio was based on valuations as of March 31, 2025, and adjusted for subsequent capital calls and distributions.

Since Fitch's last review, fund performance has continued to improve, with portfolio distributions of $45 million since Fitch's last review and slight upticks in fund NAV. The class A and B notes' loan-to-value (LTV) ratios have decreased to 40% and 65%, respectively from 50% and 75% at the last review in Dec. 2024. The transaction has entered its turbo amortization period as of Feb. 2025 and all available cash, after calls, fees, expenses, and interest, are directed to the repayments of the class A and B notes, sequentially.

However, the transaction's liquidity is somewhat constrained while distributions are low, with primary liquidity sources to meet uncalled capital commitments, service debt and cover expenses limited to the liquidity facility and distributions from underlying funds.

Fitch expects portfolio NAV appreciation and distributions to remain somewhat limited, driven by the transaction's exposure to funds focused on the buyout and venture capital sectors, which have been impacted by the weaker exit environment and the recovery timeline remains uncertain.

KEY RATING DRIVERS

The rating affirmation of the undrawn liquidity facility reflects its senior position in the capital structure and low loan to value (LTV) of approximately 16%, if fully drawn. The Stable Outlook for the undrawn liquidity facility reflects Fitch's expectation that the facility, if drawn, can withstand Fitch's stress scenarios at the current rating category.

The upgrade of the class A notes to 'BBB+sf' reflects the positive underlying fund performance and the notes' ability to withstand Fitch's stress scenarios in the fourth-quartile launch year scenario in Fitch's base case modeling. Conversely, the notes continue to exhibit a high degree of sensitivity in Fitch's rating sensitivity analysis testing for valuation and distribution declines. Fitch accounted for this, as well as high relative leverage and weaker past performance, in its qualitative assessment which resulted in a two-notch adjustment to the 'Asf' model-implied rating.

The Positive Outlook for the class A notes reflects the expectation for further improvement as the notes are in their turbo amortization phase.

The upgrade of the class B notes to 'BBsf' reflects the notes' ability to pass the third-quartile, 'BBBsf' rating level scenarios but with significant sensitivity in rating stresses with any material NAV or distribution reduction, thereby resulting in a qualitative adjustment of three notches from the 'BBBsf' model-implied rating. The class B notes had an LTV ratio of approximately 65% of NAV as of July 31, 2025, a significant drop from 74% at the Dec. 2024 review. The Stable Outlook on the class B notes reflects the likelihood of the LTV continuing to improve as the transaction de-levers, but principal payments to class B notes not occurring until senior notes are fully redeemed.

Key structural protections include amortization triggers tied to LTV levels, a liquidity facility to cover class A interest, expenses, and capital calls in the event of liquidity gaps, and long final maturities on the bonds to allow the structure additional time to potentially weather down markets.

In the 12 months through July 31, 2025, the transaction's liquidity needs included approximately $2 million in capital calls, $1 million in expenses, and $5 million in class A and class B note interest, totaling $8 million. Over the same period, liquidity sources included $20 million of capacity available on the liquidity facility, and $45 million of cash from distributions. Based on these figures, Fitch calculates the transaction's liquidity coverage ratio for a period of 12 months at 7.9x.

An additional potential liquidity source is Nassau 2019's right to require affiliates of Nassau to contribute capital to the issuer to satisfy capital calls when there is a cash shortfall. However, Fitch did not consider this potential capital contribution in its analysis, because Fitch lacks a formal credit view of Nassau or its parent, NAI. A capital contribution by the sponsor would support the transaction.

Fitch believes the manager (NAI) has sufficient capabilities and resources required to manage this transaction. NAI's management team has extensive experience, although it is comparably smaller than the managers of other Fitch-rated PE CFOs.

The sponsor and noteholders' interests are aligned, as the sponsor and its affiliates hold the equity stake and a portion of the class B notes in Nassau 2019.

Fitch has a rating cap at the 'Asf' category for PE CFO transactions, primarily driven by the uncertain nature of alternative investment fund cash flows.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--The liquidity facility could be downgraded if it is drawn, and the transaction's liquidity position is expected to deteriorate materially. However, Fitch does not view this as likely in the near-term given the low-LTV of the liquidity facility (if fully drawn) and resiliency under Fitch's 'Asf' stress scenarios.

--The ratings assigned to the notes may be sensitive to actual cash flows coming in lower than model projections, creating an increased risk that the funds will not generate enough overall cash to repay the noteholders, or pay for capital calls, expenses, and interest on time.

The class A notes would be fully repaid assuming an additional 5% distribution haircut, with approximately 15% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year. However, in a 15% distribution haircut scenario, the class A notes could be downgraded to below 'BBsf'. Given the class B notes rating sensitivity in Fitch's all quartile rating scenario, as described above, the notes are more susceptible to a rating downgrade in the event of sustained distribution shortfalls.

--The ratings assigned to the notes may be also sensitive to additional declines in NAV that Fitch believes indicate insufficient forthcoming cash distributions to support the notes at the current rating level stress. The class A notes would be fully repaid, at the 'BBBsf' rating level, assuming an additional 20% NAV haircut, with 15% of collateral value remaining in the worst launch year. However, in a 30% NAV haircut scenario, the class A notes could be downgraded to below 'BBsf'. Given the class B notes rating sensitivity in Fitch's all quartile rating scenario, as described above, the notes are more susceptible to a rating downgrade in the event of NAV depreciation.

--The ratings assigned to the notes are also sensitive to the financial health of the transaction's counterparties. A rating downgrade of a counterparty may be linked to and materially affect the ratings on the notes, given the reliance of the issuer on counterparties to provide functions, including providers of the liquidity facility and bank accounts.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--The class A notes could be upgraded to 'Asf' if they pass Fitch's 'Asf' rating stresses with sufficient cushion to downside scenarios for more than a temporary period.

--The class B notes could be upgraded to 'BBBsf' if they pass Fitch's 'BBBsf' rating stresses with sufficient cushion to downside scenarios for more than a temporary period.

--Fitch has an 'A' category rating cap for PE CFOs. Therefore, positive rating sensitivities are not applicable for the undrawn liquidity facility.

Best/Worst Case Rating Scenario

International scale credit ratings of Structured Finance transactions have a best-case rating upgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a positive direction) of seven notches over a three-year rating horizon; and a worst-case rating downgrade scenario (defined as the 99th percentile of rating transitions, measured in a negative direction) of seven notches over three years. The complete span of best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings for all rating categories ranges from 'AAAsf' to 'Dsf'. Best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings are based on historical performance. For more information about the methodology used to determine sector-specific best- and worst-case scenario credit ratings, visit https://www. tchratings.com/site/re/10111579.

USE OF THIRD PARTY DUE DILIGENCE PURSUANT TO SEC RULE 17G -10

Form ABS Due Diligence-15E was not provided to, or reviewed by, Fitch in relation to this rating action.

DATA ADEQUACY

As the timing and size of the cash flows is uncertain, Fitch used historical private equity fund performance data from a well-known third-party data provider, which covers all performance quartiles of the various fund strategies and vintages ranging from 1990 to 2025, to model expected distributions, capital calls and NAVs of the private equity funds.

data.title: Major League Baseball Signs New Rights Deals with ESPN, NBCU and Netflix — WSJ
data.description:
By Isabella Simonetti and Joe Flint

Major League Baseball has completed new three-year media rights agreements with ESPN, NBCUniversal and Netflix, the league is set to announce Wednesday.

Disney's ESPN will pay around $550 million a year for a broad deal including the rights to sell and distribute the league's out-of-market streaming service MLB.TV. The deal includes a package of 30 national games per season, rights to air Memorial Day game coverage, the Little League Classic and the rights to sell and distribute the MLB Network, as well as in-market games for select teams.

Earlier this year, ESPN and MLB agreed to end their television deal after the 2025 season. ESPN was due to pay the league $550 million a year from 2026 to 2028 for a package that included the right to air 30 regular season games per season, as well as the annual Home Run Derby and Wild Card playoff round.

NBCUniversal, owned by Comcast, will pay around $200 million a year for Sunday Night Baseball on NBC and all four Wild Card series of the postseason. Some games will also appear exclusively on NBCU's streaming service, Peacock, and its recently launched cable sports channel NBCSN.

For NBCU, the agreement means it will have sports on Sunday night throughout the calendar year. It already carries National Football League and National Basketball Association games there.

Netflix will pay around $50 million a year for rights to air a single game on the season's opening night, as well as the Home Run Derby and the annual "MLB at Field of Dreams" game.

The Wall Street Journal previously reported the companies were closing in on the agreements.

Write to Isabella Simonetti at isabella.simonetti@wsj.com and Joe Flint at Joe.Flint@wsj.com
